docs.text: Trio-Engineio ☛ The trio asynchronous Engine.IO client. Trio-Engineio is an asynchronous Engine.IO client using the trio framework. Engine.IO is the implementation of a robust bidirectional communication layer for Socket.IO . Start by reading the introductory tutorial , then check the User's Guide for more information. The rest of the docs describes each component of Trio-Engineio in detail, with a full reference in the API section. Finally, you may find some Additional notes Trio-Engineio depends on the trio asynchronous framework. Refer to trio documentation for usage information. Getting Started User's Guide API Reference Additional notes Info Only the revision 3 of the Engine.IO protocol is supported. It allows connection to a v2.x Socket.IO server.
docs.title: Welcome to Trio-engineio

docs.text: Changelog ☛ What's new in this version? All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file. The format is based on Keep a Changelog , and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning . Unreleased ☛ 0.1.1 - 2022-12-03 ☛ Fixed ☛ Import error when trying to import trio-typing. It is now only imported for static type checking in develeopment environment. Changed ☛ __version__ is no more a public attribute of the package Documentation ☛ Create the library documentation site on GitHub Pages Miscellaneous ☛ Fixed regression on isort config when running on GitHub Actions The Changelog format is now based on Keep a Changelog Developper tasks runner use the Invoke library instead of dedicated bash scripts Project dependencies are now managed through GitHub Dependabot Bump httpcore from 0.16.0 to 0.16.1 The usage example "tets_trio_client.py" is now in a dedicated "Examples" folder 0.1.0 - 2022-11-13 ☛ First release.
docs.title: Changelog

Credits ☛ Many thanks to.... Trio-engineio is based on python-engineio from Miguel Grinberg. If you don't need a trio integration or if you need an Engine.IO server, please use this awesome library! Trio-Engineio implementation uses these projects: Thanks you! Python , a programming language that lets you work quickly and integrate systems more effectively. trio , a friendly Python library for async concurrency and I/O. httpcore , the HTTP Core package provides a minimal low-level HTTP client, which does one thing only. Sending HTTP requests. trio-websocket , a WebSocket implementation for the Trio framework that strives for safety, correctness, and ergonomics. The Trio-Engineio documentation follows the best practice for project documentation as described by Daniele Procida in the Diátaxis documentation framework .
docs.title: Credits

docs.text: Additional notes ☛ About Engine.IO and Trio-Engineio... Why another Engine.IO client in Python? ☛ When I look for a Python implementation of a Engine.IO client for one of my personal project, I naturally found the python-socketio from Miguel Grinberg. This library supports the standard asyncio framework but not the trio framework which was a strong requirement for me. After some infructuous research on the Web, it appears that I will have to do my own port of the python-socketio client to trio ( 1 ). As Engine.IO is the low-level transport engine of the Socket.IO protocol, my firts task was to port the python-engineio client to trio: Trio-Engineio was born... and a Trio-Socketio library is comming!
docs.title: Additional notes

docs.text: About Engine.IO and Trio-Engineio...

docs.text: User's Guide ☛ How-to's and Recipes... How To install Trio-Engineio? ☛ Python Version ☛ We recommend using the latest version of Python. Trio-Engineio supports Python 3.7.2 and newer. Dependencies ☛ These libraries will be installed automatically when installing Trio-Engineio. trio , to manage async concurrency and I/O, but it is for sure yet install in your project environment if you install Trio-Engineio! httpcore , to manage a pool of async HTTP connections and send HTTP requests in the long-polling transport mode. trio-websocket , to connect to the server and send / receive message in the Websocket transport mode. Install Trio-Engineio in a virtual environment ☛ Use a virtual environment to manage the dependencies for your project. You may use the standard python venv module or your preferred packages manager such as poetry . Python venv Poetry Create an environment: $ mkdir myproject $ cd myproject $ python3 -m venv venv Tip On Windows, you may have to replace the python3 command by py -3 , depending on your Python installation. Activate the environment: $ source venv/bin/activate or, on Windows: $ venv/Scripts/activate.bat Install Trio-Engineio: $ pip install trio_engineio Setup a new project: $ poetry new --src myproject Add a dependency to Trio-Engineio and install it: $ poetry add trio_engineio $ poetry install Activate the project environment created by Poetry: $ source { path_to_venv } /bin/activate or, on Windows: $ { path_to_venv } /Scripts/activate.bat Tip You can retrieve the {path_to_venv} created by Poetry with: $ poetry env info --path
docs.title: User's Guide

docs.text: API Reference ☛ If you are looking for information on a specific function, class or method, this part of the documentation is for you. The public API of Trio-Engineio provides one EngineIoClient class and the EngineIoConnectionError exception. EngineIoClient ☛ An Engine.IO client for trio. This class implements an asynchronous Engine.IO client with support for websocket and long-polling transports, compatible with the revision 3 of Engine.IO protocol and the trio framework. PARAMETER DESCRIPTION logger The logging configuration. Possible values are: False to disable logging. Note that fatal errors will still be logged. True to enable logging at INFO log level. a custom logging.Logger object. TYPE: logging . Logger | bool DEFAULT: False json An alternate json module to use for encoding and decoding packets. Custom json modules must have dumps and loads functions that are compatible with the standard library versions. TYPE: JsonProtocol | None DEFAULT: None request_timeout A timeout in seconds for requests. The default is 5 seconds. TYPE: float DEFAULT: 5.0 http_session an initialized httpcore.AsyncConnectionPool object to be used when sending requests to the server. Use it if you need to add special client options such as proxy servers, SSL certificates, etc. TYPE: httpcore . AsyncConnectionPool | None DEFAULT: None ssl_verify True to verify SSL certificates, or False to skip SSL certificate verification, allowing connections to servers with self-signed certificates. The default is True . TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True ATTRIBUTE DESCRIPTION state A string representing the state of the Engine.IO connection. Possible values are: "disconnected", at initialization or after connection closing. "connected", after a successful connection handshake. "disconnecting", transient state during connection closing. TYPE: str event_names class-attribute ☛ event_names : ClassVar [ tuple [ EventName , ... ]] = get_args ( EventName ) A tuple of authorized keys to identify an event handlers (Class attribute). on ☛ on ( event , handler = None ) Register an event handler. PARAMETER DESCRIPTION event The event name. Can be "connect", "message" or "disconnect". TYPE: EventName handler The function that should be invoked to handle the event. When this parameter is not given, the method acts as a decorator for the handler function. TYPE: Callable [[ Any ], Any ] | None DEFAULT: None RAISES DESCRIPTION ValueError Raised if event is not a valid event name. Examples: As a decorator: @eio . on ( 'connect' ) def connect_handler (): print ( 'Connection request' ) As a method: def message_handler ( msg ): print ( 'Received message: ' , msg ) eio . send ( 'response' ) eio . on ( 'message' , message_handler ) connect async ☛ connect ( nursery , url , headers = None , transports = None , engineio_path = b "/engine.io" ) Connect to an Engine.IO server. PARAMETER DESCRIPTION nursery a trio.Nursery object in which to run the Engine.IO background tasks. TYPE: trio . Nursery url The URL of the Engine.IO server. It can include custom query string parameters if required by the server. TYPE: httpcore . URL | bytes | str headers An optional dictionary with custom headers to send with the connection request. TYPE: HeadersAsMapping | HeadersAsSequence | None DEFAULT: None transports A sequence of allowed transports. Valid transports are "polling" and "websocket". If not given, the polling transport is connected first, then an upgrade to websocket is attempted. TYPE: Transport | Sequence [ Transport ] | None DEFAULT: None engineio_path The endpoint where the Engine.IO server is installed. The default value is appropriate for most cases. TYPE: str | bytes DEFAULT: b'/engine.io' RAISES DESCRIPTION EngineIoConnectionError Raised if called while the Engine.IO client is not in a "disconnected" state or if no valid transports are provided or if either url or headers or engineio_path type are incorrect. RETURNS DESCRIPTION bool True if the connection succeeded, False otherwise. Examples: eio = EngineIoClient () async with trio . open_nursery () as nursery : await eio . connect ( nursery , "http://127.0.0.1:1234" ) send async ☛ send ( data , binary = None ) Send a message to a client. Do nothing if the client is not connected. PARAMETER DESCRIPTION data The data to send to the client. Data can be of type str , bytes , list or dict . If a list or dict , the data will be serialized as JSON. TYPE: str | bytes | list [ Any ] | dict [ Any , Any ] binary True to send packet as binary, False to send as text. If not given, str are sent as text and bytes are sent as binary. TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None disconnect async ☛ disconnect () Disconnect from the server. transport ☛ transport () Return the name of the transport currently in use. RETURNS DESCRIPTION Transport | None Either "polling" or "websocket" when the client is connected or None otherwise. sleep async ☛ sleep ( seconds = 0.0 ) Sleep for the requested amount of time. PARAMETER DESCRIPTION seconds sleep duration in seconds. TYPE: float DEFAULT: 0.0 EngineIOError ☛ Bases: Exception Base class for the trio_engineio exceptions. EngineIoConnectionError ☛ Bases: EngineIOError Exception raised when connection to the server failed.
docs.title: API Reference

True to enable logging at INFO log level. a custom logging.Logger object. TYPE: logging . Logger | bool DEFAULT: False json An alternate json module to use for encoding and decoding packets. Custom json modules must have dumps and loads functions that are compatible with the standard library versions. TYPE: JsonProtocol | None DEFAULT: None request_timeout A timeout in seconds for requests. The default is 5 seconds. Use it if you need to add special client options such as proxy servers, SSL certificates, etc. TYPE: httpcore . The default is True . TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True ATTRIBUTE DESCRIPTION state A string representing the state of the Engine.IO connection. Possible values are: "disconnected", at initialization or after connection closing. "connected", after a successful connection handshake. "disconnecting", transient state during connection closing. It can include custom query string parameters if required by the server. TYPE: httpcore . URL | bytes | str headers An optional dictionary with custom headers to send with the connection request. If not given, the polling transport is connected first, then an upgrade to websocket is attempted. RETURNS DESCRIPTION bool True if the connection succeeded, False otherwise. docs.text: Getting started ☛ You're at the right place to start! What is Engine.IO? ☛ Engine.IO is a transport protocol that enables event-based bidirectional communication between clients and a server. The official implementations of the client and server components are written in JavaScript. python-engineio is a complete Python implementation of both, each with standard and asyncio variants. This library provides a Python implementations of the client only, but using the trio asynchronous framework. The Engine.IO protocol is extremely simple. Once a connection between a client and a server is established, either side can send “messages” to the other side. Event handlers provided by the applications on both ends are invoked when a message is received, or when a connection is established or dropped. Installing trio-engineio ☛ trio-engineio requires Python 3.7.2 or greater to be installed on your computer. Install trio-engineio using pip or your favorite Python packages manager: Linux / macOS Windows $ python3 -m pip install trio_engineio $ py -3 -m pip install trio_engineio Creating a client instance ☛ To instantiate an Engine.IO client, simply create an instance of the EngineIoClient class: test_trio_client.py from trio_engineio import EngineIoClient eio = EngineIoClient () Registering event Handlers ☛ Any function decorated with the EngineIoClient.on decorator is registered as an event handler and is called when the corresponding event is triggered by the server. The decorator's argument is the name of the event. Three events are supported: connect message disconnect test_trio_client.py @eio . on ( "connect" ) def on_connect (): print ( "Connected!" ) @eio . on ( "message" ) def on_message ( data ): print ( f "Received a message: { data } " ) @eio . on ( "disconnect" ) def on_disconnect (): print ( "Disconnected!" ) The "message" event handler shall accept a unique argument, allowing the server to pass application-specific data with the message. The "connect" and "message" events are always sent by the server, but the "disconnect" event can be raised on client initiated disconnection, server initiated disconnection or unexpected disconnection, for example due to a network failure. Event handlers can be regular functions as above, or can also be coroutines: test_trio_client.py @eio . on ( "message" ) async def on_message ( data ): print ( f "Received a message: { data } " ) Connecting to a server ☛ The connection to a server is performed asynchronously using the EngineIoClient.connect coroutine. As it launches several background trio tasks to keep the connection alive and handle incoming events, it requires a trio nursery to host them. The second argument of EngineIoClient.connect is the url of an active Engine.IO server. You should take care of the EngineIoConnectionError exception. test_trio_client.py import trio from trio_engineio import EngineIoConnectionError async def main (): async with trio . open_nursery () as nursery : try : await eio . connect ( nursery , "http://127.0.0.1:3000" ) except EngineIoConnectionError : print ( "Connection refused by the server!" ) trio . run ( main ) Sending messages ☛ The client can send a message to the server by calling the EngineIoClient.send coroutine and providing it with the data to be passed to the server. str , bytes , dict or list are accepted. Data included in dictionaries and lists shall also be of these types. await eio . send ({ "foo" : "bar" }) You can call the EngineIoClient.send coroutine inside an event handler to answer to a server event or in any other part of your application. Disconnecting from the server ☛ The client can disconnect from the server using the EngineIoClient.disconnect coroutine. It will trigger any registered "disconnect" event handler. await eio . disconnect () Debugging and Troubleshooting ☛ The EngineIoClient constructor accepts a "logger" argument to help you debug issues into your application such as connection problems, unexpected disconnection,... Possible values are: False to disable logging. Note that fatal errors will still be logged on stderr. True to enable logging at INFO log level on stderr. a custom logging.Logger object. from trio_engineio import EngineIoClient eio = EngineIoClient ( logger = True )
docs.title: Getting started

docs.text: You're at the right place to start!

docs.text: Engine.IO is a transport protocol that enables event-based bidirectional communication between clients and a server. The official implementations of the client and server components are written in JavaScript. python-engineio is a complete Python implementation of both, each with standard and asyncio variants. This library provides a Python implementations of the client only, but using the trio asynchronous framework. The Engine.IO protocol is extremely simple. Once a connection between a client and a server is established, either side can send “messages” to the other side. Event handlers provided by the applications on both ends are invoked when a message is received, or when a connection is established or dropped.
docs.title: What is Engine.IO?

docs.text: trio-engineio requires Python 3.7.2 or greater to be installed on your computer. Install trio-engineio using pip or your favorite Python packages manager: Linux / macOS Windows $ python3 -m pip install trio_engineio $ py -3 -m pip install trio_engineio
docs.title: Installing trio-engineio

docs.text: To instantiate an Engine.IO client, simply create an instance of the EngineIoClient class: test_trio_client.py from trio_engineio import EngineIoClient eio = EngineIoClient ()
docs.title: Creating a client instance

docs.text: Any function decorated with the EngineIoClient.on decorator is registered as an event handler and is called when the corresponding event is triggered by the server. The decorator's argument is the name of the event. Three events are supported: connect message disconnect test_trio_client.py @eio . on ( "connect" ) def on_connect (): print ( "Connected!" ) @eio . on ( "message" ) def on_message ( data ): print ( f "Received a message: { data } " ) @eio . on ( "disconnect" ) def on_disconnect (): print ( "Disconnected!" ) The "message" event handler shall accept a unique argument, allowing the server to pass application-specific data with the message. The "connect" and "message" events are always sent by the server, but the "disconnect" event can be raised on client initiated disconnection, server initiated disconnection or unexpected disconnection, for example due to a network failure. Event handlers can be regular functions as above, or can also be coroutines: test_trio_client.py @eio . on ( "message" ) async def on_message ( data ): print ( f "Received a message: { data } " )
docs.title: Registering event Handlers

docs.text: The connection to a server is performed asynchronously using the EngineIoClient.connect coroutine. As it launches several background trio tasks to keep the connection alive and handle incoming events, it requires a trio nursery to host them. The second argument of EngineIoClient.connect is the url of an active Engine.IO server. You should take care of the EngineIoConnectionError exception. test_trio_client.py import trio from trio_engineio import EngineIoConnectionError async def main (): async with trio . open_nursery () as nursery : try : await eio . connect ( nursery , "http://127.0.0.1:3000" ) except EngineIoConnectionError : print ( "Connection refused by the server!" ) trio . run ( main )
docs.title: Connecting to a server

docs.text: The client can send a message to the server by calling the EngineIoClient.send coroutine and providing it with the data to be passed to the server. str , bytes , dict or list are accepted. Data included in dictionaries and lists shall also be of these types. await eio . send ({ "foo" : "bar" }) You can call the EngineIoClient.send coroutine inside an event handler to answer to a server event or in any other part of your application.
docs.title: Sending messages

docs.text: The client can disconnect from the server using the EngineIoClient.disconnect coroutine. It will trigger any registered "disconnect" event handler. await eio . disconnect ()
docs.title: Disconnecting from the server

docs.text: The EngineIoClient constructor accepts a "logger" argument to help you debug issues into your application such as connection problems, unexpected disconnection,... Possible values are: False to disable logging. Note that fatal errors will still be logged on stderr. True to enable logging at INFO log level on stderr. a custom logging.Logger object. from trio_engineio import EngineIoClient eio = EngineIoClient ( logger = True )
docs.title: Debugging and Troubleshooting
